coredata.dc:title: A facile, green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Polyalthia longifolia and its application for the selective detection of cadmium
coredata.dc:creator: Sariga,
Ayilliath Kolaprath, Mrinalini Kalyani
Benny, Libina
Varghese, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has received world-wide recognition for their outstanding physicochemical properties that have the ability to substitute the semiconductor quantum dots. Herein, we have developed a strategy to determine the presence of Cd2+ using CQDs as a fluorescence probe. The CQDs were synthesized from the leaves of Polyalthia longifolia (a natural source) through a one-step hydrothermal method. The CQDs obtained from Polyalthia longifolia (p-CQDs) was characterized using XRD, TEM, FTIR, Raman Spectroscopy, XPS Studies, UV–Visible spectroscopy and PL Spectroscopy. The p-CQDs displayed bright red fluorescence under the UV light, with good water solubility, and appreciable photostability and a quantum yield of 22%. The p-CQDs had a quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size of 3.33 nm. The p-CQDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cd2+ with a low limit of detection of 2.4 nM and a wide linear range of 7.3 nM–12 μM. The PL intensity of the p-CQDs showed a quenching effect in presence of Cd2+ and the mechanism of quenching was validated via fluorescence lifetime decay studies. We have also studied the effectiveness of the fluorescent probe developed for Cd2+ sensing in real samples of ground water and industrial effluents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal synthesis
Cadmium sensing
Fluorescence quenching
Industrial effluents

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Cd2+ ions
Analysis of Cd2+ in various water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of p-CQDs (b) Histogram particle size distribution of p-CQDs.
(a) XRD pattern of the p-CQDs (b) Raman spectrum of the p-CQDs (c) FT-IR spectrum of the p-CQDs.
XPS spectrum of p-CQDs (a) Survey spectra (b) C1s spectrum (c) O1s spectrum (d) N1s spectrum.
(a) Absorbance spectrum of p-CQDs (b) Emission spectrum of p-CQDs (c) Excitation wavelength-dependent PL spectrum (d) Area normalized excitation dependent emission spectrum of p-CQDs.

(a) Effect of ionic strength on the luminescence intensity of p-CQDs (b) Effect of pH on the luminescence intensity of p-CQDs (c) Effect of UV irradiation on the luminescence intensity of p-CQDs (d) Long-term luminescence stability of p-CQDs.
Zeta potential of p-CQDs at pH 7.

(a) PL intensity of p-CQDs in the presence of Cd2+ ions at different concentrations from 0 to 12 μM (b) plot of F0/F with the concentration of Cd2+ (c) FT-IR spectrum of the p-CQDs after Cd2+ adsorption.
XPS spectrum of p-CQDs (a) Survey spectra (b) C1s spectrum (c) O1s spectrum (d) N1s spectrum Cd2+ adsorption.
(a) PL decay of p-CQDs in the presence and absence of Cd2+ ions at the excitation wavelength of 540 nm (b) PL intensity of p-CQDs toward various metal ions in the absence and presence of Cd2+ ions.
General representation of the synthesis of polyalthia derived CQDs and its sensing mechanism towards toxic Cd2+ ions in ground water and industrial waste water samples.
Comparison of the proposed method with reported CQDs based probes for Cd2+ sensing.
Analysis of Cd2+ in real samples of ground water and industrial waste water.

row.entry: Obtained Conc. by the proposed method a (mg/mL)

head.ce:title: A facile, green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Polyalthia longifolia and its application for the selective detection of cadmium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka, 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka, 560029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has received world-wide recognition for their outstanding physicochemical properties that have the ability to substitute the semiconductor quantum dots. Herein, we have developed a strategy to determine the presence of Cd2+ using CQDs as a fluorescence probe. The CQDs were synthesized from the leaves of Polyalthia longifolia (a natural source) through a one-step hydrothermal method. The CQDs obtained from Polyalthia longifolia (p-CQDs) was characterized using XRD, TEM, FTIR, Raman Spectroscopy, XPS Studies, UV–Visible spectroscopy and PL Spectroscopy. The p-CQDs displayed bright red fluorescence under the UV light, with good water solubility, and appreciable photostability and a quantum yield of 22%. The p-CQDs had a quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size of 3.33 nm. The p-CQDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cd2+ with a low limit of detection of 2.4 nM and a wide linear range of 7.3 nM–12 μM. The PL intensity of the p-CQDs showed a quenching effect in presence of Cd2+ and the mechanism of quenching was validated via fluorescence lifetime decay studies. We have also studied the effectiveness of the fluorescent probe developed for Cd2+ sensing in real samples of ground water and industrial effluents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic pollutants causing water contamination is one of the global issues that has consistently grown over time. They possess high risks to health, ecological balance, and biodiversity. Among the inorganic pollutants, the presence of heavy metals in the water bodies has drastically increased. Human activities including excessive usage of agrochemicals, mining, and industrial wastewater are the major causes of the seepage of heavy metals into the water bodies. Natural phenomena such as volcanic eruption and rock weathering also add to this cause. Heavy metals like Cu, Cd, Cr, As, Hg, etc., in soil do not have the ability to degrade. Hence, they remain in the soil as pollutants for a long time and are harmful to plant and animal life. Cadmium is one of the most toxic and hazardous heavy metals which is widely employed in nickel-cadmium batteries, plastic coloration, and cigarettes. Cadmium shows the highest transfer to plants and destroys their cell membranes, biomolecules, and organelles, and adversely affects plant metabolisms. Unlike many other heavy metals, cadmium has no part in biological processes. Therefore, its presence even in very low concentrations can lead to adverse effects in living organisms through bioaccumulation. Higher doses of cadmium may result in kidney damage, and lung and prostate cancer in humans. Also, cadmium was the cause of itai-itai disease in Japan [1–4].

Several rapid and sensitive sensing methods have been developed to determine the presence of these pollutants, both qualitatively and quantitatively. Heavy metal sensing can be performed through electronics, electrochemical or optical studies. Among the several optical methods, fluorescence studies have gained importance because of their excellent sensitivity and specificity, cost-effectiveness, faster response time, and technical simplicity. Certain limitations of fluorescence studies include their low quantum yield and fabrication difficulties [5,6]. Several nanomaterials like MOFs, polymers, metal nanoparticles, semiconductor oxides, chalcogenides, quantum dots and so on have been employed as metal detector probes throughout the years. Among these, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are highly preferred for fluorescence sensing studies due to their eco-friendliness, ease of synthesis, water-solubility, as well as good conductivity. CQDs are mainly synthesized via hydrothermal treatment, laser ablation, pyrolysis, oxidation processes, microwave irradiation, and electrochemical methods. They are also non-toxic and biocompatible in nature possessing superior and tunable photoluminescence properties [7–10]. Besides their photochemical stability, CQDs can also be easily surface functionalized or passivized using several organic, inorganic, biological, and polymeric substances, including epoxy, amino, hydroxamic, and carboxyl groups so as to create sites with high selectivity for the heavy metal sensing. They can also be doped with several metallic elements like Ru-CQDs, lanthanide-doped CQDs, and heavy metal-doped CQDs, as well as non-metallic heteroatoms like Iodine-doped CQDs and Nitrogen-doped CQDs, to effectively improve their surface chemistry and modify their intrinsic electronic and optical properties for various applications. Extensive application of such modified CQDs for multicolor bioimaging has been reported [11–13]. CQDs exhibit fluorescence properties owing to the quantum confinement effect, surface imperfections, and bandgap transitions of conjugated p-domains [9,14,15]. A recent article showed high-temperature resistance of CQDs up to 800 °C, thus broadening its applications in elevated temperature environments [16].

CQDs are mainly synthesized via top-down or bottom-up approaches. Bottom-up synthesis techniques are generally preferred where-in small organic molecules are heated above their boiling point (via hydrothermal treatment). It was observed that such approaches, despite being cumbersome, yielded CQDs with high quantum yields [17]. Hydrothermal synthesis follows a simple, ecologically sound, one-step procedure. It requires mild reaction conditions and shows high dispersion in solution [18]. The common carbon precursors for the synthesis of CQDs via hydrothermal method include citric acid, thiourea, and tetraphenyl porphyrin [10–13]. The quantum yield was further found to increase when the CQDs are synthesized from biomass waste sources. This renewable strategy of biomass-derived CQD synthesis was an effective means to discard the waste in a sustainable manner. Numerous agricultural sources and food wastes like lemon peel, coconut husk, rice husk, bamboo leaves, lychee, and wheat straw were employed for the CQD synthesis till recent times [19,20].

Recently, such cost-effective, green synthetic strategies have gained much popularity for the development of CQDs. The obtained CQDs were found to have excellent biocompatibility and non-toxicity [21]. Research demonstrates a highly economical and green synthesis of CQDs via hydrothermal treatment from Saccharum officinarum juice. The obtained CQDs had spherical structure with a particle size of ∼3 nm and showed blue fluorescence with high water solubility. It was successfully used for the cellular imaging of Escherichia coli (bacteria) and Saccharomyces cerevisiae (yeast) [22]. The CQDs prepared from orange juice by hydrothermal treatment showed high biocompatibility, photostability, tunable photoluminescence, and low toxicity. The prepared CQDs had an average size of 1.5–4.5 nm and were used as a potential tool for cellular imaging [23]. CQDs with a particle size of 2.9 nm were synthesized from the orange pericarp via the hydrothermal method. Appreciable stability, good water-solubility, and narrow particle size distribution of the CQDs adds to its potential applications in nano-biotechnology [24]. Fabrication of blue fluorescent CQDs having a particle size of 2–4 nm was achieved through the hydrothermal technique using pomelo peel as the precursor. It was successfully employed for the sensitive determination of Hg2+ in lake water with a low detection limit of 0.23 nM [25]. The formation of bright yellow fluorescent CQDs through hydrothermal synthesis was reported using aloe as the carbon precursor. They had a spherical morphology with an average size of 5 nm. The obtained CQDs with abundant hydroxyl functionalities showed good solubility, photostability, and low toxicity. The CQDs were applied for the sensing of tartrazine, and also showed extended utilization in bioimaging and drug delivery [26]. Though these reports reveal various sensing applications of CQDs, the application of biomass-derived CQDs for cadmium sensing is still unexplored.

In this work, we report a red-emitting fluorescence probe based on CQDs for the determination of the heavy metal, cadmium. The CQDs were synthesized from the leaves of Polyalthia longifolia via a hydrothermal (bottom-up) approach. The CQDs obtained from Polyalthia longifolia (p-CQDs) displayed high water solubility, excellent photostability, and a good quantum yield of 22%. The prepared fluorescence probe showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cd2+ detection in a varying range of concentrations of the analyte. To examine the practical application of the sensor, analysis of Cd2+ ions in tap water and industrial wastewater samples was performed with satisfactory results (Scheme 1 ).

Polyalthia longifolia leaves were collected from Christ University (Bangalore, India). Cadmium chloride, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) were purchased from SD Fine-Chem Pvt Ltd., India. Also, metal salts of K+, Na+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ag2+, Co2+, Mn2+, and Hg2+ were purchased from SD Fine-Chem Pvt Ltd., India. All the reagents were of pure analytical grade. Deionized water was used for all solution preparations throughout the study.

The FT-IR spectrum was obtained using Thermo Nicolet, Avatar 370 for the detection of surface functional groups of the carbon quantum dots. Powder XRD patterns were monitored using Rigaku SmartLab X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). TEM images for the analysis of the morphology of the p-CQDs were obtained from JEOL (JEM-F200) electron microscope at a 200 kV accelerating potential. XPS data was identified with the ESCA-14 (Omicron Nanotechnology) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Raman spectrum was studied using Lab RAM HR FT-Raman module at 532 nm LASER. The absorption spectra were recorded by employing the Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–visible 1800). All fluorescence measurements such as emission, excitation and PL were investigated using the Shimadzu spectrofluorometer (RF5301PC). PBS buffer solution was used for all the experimental investigations at a pH of 7.

The p-CQDs was synthesized via a typical bottom-up approach using hydrothermal method. The Polyalthia leaves were collected, washed thoroughly with deionized water and dried in sunlight for 5 h. The dried leaves were dispersed in deionized water and subjected to ultrasonication. The solution was filtered and then transferred into a Teflon autoclave. The autoclave was heated in a muffle furnace at 150 °C for 6 h. The reactor was then cooled to room temperature and the product was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was filtered using Whatman 40 and further purified using column chromatography. The synthesized p-CQDs was stored in its solution form at 4 °C for further studies, characterizations and sensing applications.

The QY of the p-CQDs was calculated by the representative slope way with a fluorescent dye rhodamine (QA = 95%) as the reference sample. The calculations were performed using the equation below.
(1) QB = QA (IB/IA) (nB/nA)2
Where QA and QB represents fluorescence QY of reference sample and experimental sample, I stands for slope of the fluorescence curve and n is the refractive index.

The fluorescence stability of p-CQDs was inspected in response to different ionic strengths, pH ranges, UV irradiation and life-time assessments. The Photoluminescence data were obtained using a spectrofluorometer on excitation at 540 nm.

Sensing of Cd2+ was carried out using the prepared p-CQDs via fluorescence quenching mechanism, at room temperature by the standard addition method. Aqueous solutions containing 10 μM of p-CQDs with different concentrations of Cd2+ ranging from 7.3 nM to 12 μM was taken to investigate the quenching mechanism of the p-CQDs in the presence of Cd2+. The PL spectra was recorded using spectrofluorometer at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm. A calibration plot with concentration of Cd2+ vs fluorescence intensity was drawn for the determination of Cd2+.

The selectivity of the prepared p-CQDs towards Cd2+ detection was evaluated in presence of several interfering metal ions. The fluorescence response of p-CQDs with Cd2+ was examined separately in the absence and presence of 100 μM of different metal ions (K+, Na+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ag2+, Co2+, Mn2+, and Hg2+) at room temperature.

The p-CQDs was used for the sensing of Cd2+ in real samples from tap water and industrial waste water. The water samples after filtration, were spiked with different concentrations of Cd2+. The spiked samples were analysed for Cd2+ at room temperature by the same procedure as mentioned in section 2.6.

The p-CQDs was prepared via hydrothermal synthesis from Polyalthia leaves as explained in section 2.3. The TEM images revealed the morphology as well as size of the prepared p-CQDs [27]. The p-CQDs was found to have quasi-spherical structure with monodispersed nanoparticles. The p-CQDs showed the diameters in a narrow range of 1.5–6.5 nm, with an average crystallite size of 3.33 nm, evident from TEM results (Fig. 1 a). The histogram (Fig. 1b) demonstrates the respective size distribution for over 30 nanoparticles.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the p-CQDs reveals a single broad peak corresponding to the (002) plane at 2θ = 20.28°, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The interlayer distance obtained using the Bragg's equation is 0.43 nm, which suggests greatly disordered and amorphous carbon atoms with sp3 domain. Bragg's equation is given by,
(2) d = nλ/2sinθ
d is the interplanar distance, n is the order of diffraction, λ is the wavelength of the incident radiation, and θ is the peak position.

We obtain two peaks about between 1000 cm−1 and 1800 cm−1 from the Raman spectral data of the as-synthesized p-CQDs presented in Fig. 2b. The D band corresponds to the first peak, which is placed at 1347.69 cm−1, and the G band corresponds to the second peak, which is positioned at 1600.40 cm−1. The D band (A1g symmetry) and G band (E2g symmetry) proves the presence of disordered and graphitized carbons respectively [28]. The relative intensity of these two bands (ID/IG value) is calculated to be 1.1875.

The molecular composition and the surface functionalities of the p-CQDs can be determined using the FTIR results, as shown in Fig. 2c. The wide absorption band at 3342 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H functional groups. The absorption bands at 2926 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1 correspond to the of CH3 and CH2 groups’ stretching vibrations. The stretching vibrations of COO− and CC bonds are evident from the bands at 1610 cm−1 and 1515 cm−1 [29–31]. The presence of amino groups was further validated by the absorption bands at 1450 cm−1 and 1027 cm−1 which indicates the stretching as well as bending vibrations of N-H and C-N bonds, respectively. Also, the bands at 1247 cm−1 and 1027 cm−1 can be attributed to C-O stretching vibrations [29,32]. This reveals that the p-CQDs are highly carbonized and contain amine and hydroxyl functionalities.

XPS analysis was employed to reveal the chemical and elemental valence states of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen present in the synthesized p-CQDs. The three distinct peaks represented in the survey scan as shown in Fig. 3 a, confirm the presence of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. The C 1s core level spectrum of p-CQDs is represented in Fig. 3b. The peak at a binding energy of 284.7 eV confirms the presence of CC and C-C bond of graphitic carbon. The three distinct peaks at 286.5, 285.7 and 288.8 eV may be assigned to the C-O-C, C-N and COOH bond respectively [33]. Four deconvoluted peaks at 531.2, 532.0, 532.7 and 533.4 eV obtained from the core level O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) correspond to the C-OH, O-CO*, C-O-C and *O-CO bonds of p-CQDs. Two peaks were formed from the deconvoluted high-resolution N 1s spectrum as shown in Fig. 3d. The presence of NH-CO and C-N bond could be confirmed by peaks at 400.2 and 398.3 eV respectively. These results further support the FTIR results confirming the presence of amine, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups in p-CQDs.

The p-CQDs after purification by column chromatography displayed a light brown colour in daylight and strong red fluorescence when subjected to UV radiation. The UV–Visible spectrum of the precursor (Polyalthia leaf extract in deionized water) showed strong absorption peaks at 614 nm and 656 nm which may be attributed to the n-π* transitions of different functionalities present in the chlorophyll content of the leaves and the absorption peaks below 500 nm may be due to the π-π*transitions of different functional groups present in the precursor (Fig. S1a). The UV–Visible absorption spectrum of the purified p-CQDs reveals (Fig. 4 a) a broad spectrum in the visible range with the major peak at 540 nm. This peak may be due to the n-π* transitions of the aromatic part possessing the C-O and C-N groups. The absorption bands in the range 300–400 nm can be accredited to the π-π*transitions of the sp2 carbon domain while the absorption bands between 450 and 674 nm may be caused by the n-π* transitions of O-H, CO, or C-N groups of the aromatic core and other functionalities present in the p-CQDs (Fig. S1b) [34,35].

The photophysical properties of p-CQDs are further investigated in detail using the PL spectra which exhibits excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, as shown in Fig. 4c. On increasing the wavelength, the PL intensity of p-CQDs initially raised from 470 nm to 540 nm and then reduced, which was not observed in the emission spectrum of the precursor (Fig. S1c). The area normalized figure of the corresponding excitation dependent emission spectra as shown in Fig. 4d, exhibits the red shift behaviour of the PL maxima. The highest magnitude of PL intensity of p-CQDs was seen at a wavelength of 673 nm when excited at 540 nm (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, employing rhodamine as a standard, the QY of p-CQDs was calculated to be 22%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Cd2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The PL intensity was studied at various analyte (Cd2+) concentrations ranging from 0 μM to 12 μM and it was observed that the PL intensity was inversely related to the analyte concentration (Fig. 7 a). This demonstrated that the Cd2+ ions added, produced efficient fluorescence quenching of the p-CQDs. The quenching of fluorescence may be ascribed to the interaction of Cd2+ ions with the nitrogen or oxygen possessing functional groups present at the p-CQDs surface. The interaction between the p-CQDs and the Cd2+ ions may have caused the aggregation of the p-CQDs which would have ultimately led to the fluorescence quenching [27]. The quenching efficiency was investigated using the Stern-Volmer equation [41].
(3) F0/F = 1 + KSV [Q]
where KSV represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] represents the analyte concentration, F0 represents the fluorescence intensity in the absence of Cd2+ and F represents the fluorescence intensity in the presence of Cd2+. The KSV was calculated to be 1.06 x 103. A linear correlation (R2 = 0.991) was showed over the concentration range from 0 μM to 12 μM as shown in Fig. 7b. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined using the formula 3.3 x standard deviation/slope as given by the IUPAC standard, which was calculated to be 2.4 nM. The limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated to be 7.3 nM, using the formula 10*standard deviation/slope from the calibration plot.

The mechanism of Cd2+ binding to the p-CQDs was further examined by the FTIR and XPS data. Fig. 7c shows the FTIR pattern of the p-CQDs after the addition of the aqueous Cd2+ solution at pH 7. The spectrum reveals an increase in the peak intensity in the fingerprint region at 1610 cm−1, which can be attributed to the chemical interaction of Cd2+ with the -COO- groups of the p-CQDs [31,42]. The XPS measurements after Cd2+ adsorption is represented in Fig. 8 . The deconvoluted O1s spectra shows shifts in the binding energy peaks of O-CO* and C-O-C from 533.4 to 532.7 eV to 533.7 and 532.8 eV, respectively. This small rise could be due to the contribution of the electron density of O atoms of the p-CQDs to Cd2+ ions during the chemical interaction [43]. This suggests that some of the typical oxygen-bearing functional groups as the carboxyl groups in p-CQDs facilitates the Cd2+ adsorption. The variations in peak position of oxygen and carbon from 533.4 to 288.8 eV to 533.7 and 289.1eV, respectively indicates the state of carboxyl groups, which displays blue shift on the binding energy. These results validate the chemical bonding of Cd2+ with the oxygen-bearing functionalities of p-CQDs during the Cd2+ adsorption via the formation of carboxyl-Cd species [31].

The fluorescence lifetime measurement can differentiate the static quenching process from a dynamic quenching process. The nature of quenching was confirmed by analysing the fluorescence lifetime quenching. This study was performed in the absence and presence of Cd2+ ions of different concentrations. From Fig. 9 a, it is concluded that the Cd2+ ions did not quench the fluorescence lifetime of the p-CQDs. This indicated that there exists a ground state interaction amidst the p-CQDs and Cd2+ ions. The fluorescence life time studies showed that the quenching occurred due to the complex formed as a result of the ground state interactions between the p-CQDs and Cd2+ ions [27].

The PL properties of p-CQDs were studied in the presence and absence of different metal ions including K+, Na+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ag2+, Co2+, Mn2+, and Hg2+. Fig. 9b represents the fluorescence spectra of the p-CQDs in the presence and absence of the above-mentioned metal ions. It was inferred that the PL intensity of the p-CQDs did not exhibit much variation in the presence of metal ions other than Cd2+. Addition of Cd2+ ions caused a significant fluorescence quenching of the p-CQDs in comparison to other metal ions. This indicates that Cd2+ ions exhibit higher affinity towards the p-CQDs and gets bound to them. The cause for the fluorescence quenching was the electron transfer that occurred from p-CQD to Cd2+ ions. This proved that the as-synthesized p-CQDs showed good selectivity towards Cd2+ ions and can be used as a probe for the selective detection of Cd2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cd2+ in various water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The p-CQDs was studied for the detection of Cd2+ in real samples from tap water in addition to industrial waste water (see Table 1). The water samples were collected and were filtered using Whatman filter paper. The filtered solution was spiked with different concentrations of Cd2+ and an aliquot of it was tested for Cd2+ by employing the above-discussed method. The results confirm rapid, sensitive, efficient, and selective Cd2+ detection with a satisfactory recovery range of 98–101% Thus, the proposed sensor was successfully applied for the detection of Cd2+ in real samples. Table 2
reviews the determination of Cd2+ in ground water and industrial waste waters.

Herein, we report an environment friendly, cost-effective, one-step synthesis of highly fluorescent p-CQDs from Polyalthia leaves via hydrothermal method. The prepared p-CQDs had an average size of 3.33 nm with a quasi-spherical morphology. The p-CQDs exhibited a highly amorphous structure containing hydroxyl and amine functional groups. The aqueous solution of the obtained p-CQDs displayed strong red fluorescence on irradiation with UV light and also its PL studies revealed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, when excited at 540 nm. High water-solubility, dispersibility, long-term photochemical stability of p-CQDs along with their excellent tolerance to different ionic strengths, pH, and photobleaching adds to its potential applications, especially in sensing. The p-CQDs showed high sensitivity, selectivity, a low detection limit of 2.4 nM for the determination of Cd2+ ions. Additionally, the method was effectively employed for the analysis of Cd2+ ions in real samples of tap water and industrial effluents with an appreciable recovery of 98–101%. Thus, the results demonstrate a green, economic and energy-efficient technique for the synthesis of p-CQDs and its use as a potential tool for rapid, efficient and selective determination of Cd2+ ions.

Sariga: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mrinalini Kalyani Ayilliath Kolaprath: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Libina Benny: Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Anitha Varghese: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the Major Research Project (Major Research Project DSC-1830), Centre for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka for providing the required financial support for completing this research work.
